title: Apple sues OpenAI, Search goes AI-first, and a Chrome trick you've been ignoring
intro: Five things worth your time from July 12: Apple takes OpenAI to court over talent and trade secrets, Google makes AI the default in Search, a hidden Chrome address-bar shortcut, a 60-second passkey win, and the foldable question before Galaxy Unpacked.

posts.body: Apple is suing OpenAI — and the subtext is louder than the lawsuit. ⚖️ The complaint, filed July 11, alleges that 400+ former Apple employees now at OpenAI amount to a coordinated raid on its on-device AI, silicon, and hardware teams — not ordinary talent movement, but trade-secret theft. Honest read: suits like this usually say more about who feels behind than who's ahead. Apple's AI reboot is late, the brain drain is real, and the courtroom is where that anxiety shows up.
posts.problem: Apple's on-device AI effort has been visibly behind the rest of the industry, and it's been bleeding senior hardware and silicon talent to OpenAI for months — a slow leak that looked like normal Silicon Valley hiring until now.
posts.breakthrough: Apple filed suit against OpenAI on July 11, alleging that the 400+ former Apple employees now working there aren't just poached talent but part of a coordinated effort that carried over trade secrets from its on-device AI, silicon, and hardware teams.
posts.catch: Trade-secret suits like this are notoriously hard to win — hiring a competitor's engineers isn't illegal on its own, and courts require Apple to show specific stolen material, not just an exodus of people. Filing the suit says as much about Apple's anxiety as it does about OpenAI's conduct.
posts.forYou: Watch this less as a legal drama and more as a signal: Apple's AI reboot is running later than its own leadership wants, and the talent war for AI hardware engineers just became public and adversarial.

posts.hook: Google Search is now AI by default
posts.body: Google just quietly ended something you've used every day for 25 years. 🔎 'AI Mode,' powered by Gemini, is now the default in Search — 1B+ monthly users, with query volume doubling every quarter. Instead of ten blue links you get an AI-written answer with sources tucked inside, plus mini-tools built on the fly. Great for quick answers, genuinely disruptive for every creator, blog, and small site that lived on those clicks. The open web just got a middleman — and the time to adapt is now.
posts.problem: Ten blue links have been the default way to search the web for 25 years, and every creator, blogger, and small site built their business on the assumption that Google would keep sending people to their pages.
posts.breakthrough: Google made 'AI Mode,' powered by Gemini, the default experience in Search — over 1 billion monthly users now get an AI-written answer with sources folded in, plus on-the-fly mini-tools, instead of a ranked list of links. Query volume on it is doubling every quarter.
posts.catch: An AI-written summary answers the question without sending a click to whoever actually wrote the information — great for the person searching, and a real threat to the sites Search used to fund. Google frames it as following user behavior, not creating it, but the effect is the same either way.
posts.forYou: If your traffic, income, or attention depends on search clicks, the shift already happened — the smart move is to make your content something an AI answer engine chooses to cite, not just something a person can find.

posts.hook: Type @ in Chrome to search your own browser
posts.body: You've had this Chrome superpower the whole time and probably never used it. 💡 Type @ in the address bar and Chrome becomes a search engine for your own browser: @tabs jumps to any open tab, @history finds that article you swear you read somewhere, and @bookmarks takes you straight to a saved page. It works on Chrome 108 and up, so basically everyone already has it — no extension, no setup.
posts.problem: Chrome's address bar has quietly supported a way to search inside your own browsing activity for a while, and most people still open new tabs to hunt for a tab, a bookmark, or a page they remember reading.
posts.breakthrough: Typing @ in Chrome's address bar turns it into a search engine for your own browser — @tabs jumps straight to any open tab, @history surfaces that page you swear you read, and @bookmarks pulls up anything you saved, all without installing anything.
posts.catch: It only works from Chrome 108 onward, and it's easy to forget it exists precisely because Google never really advertised it — most people default to manually clicking through tabs instead.
posts.forYou: Next time you're hunting for a tab or a half-remembered article, type @ before you start scrolling — it's faster than it sounds and works today with zero setup.

posts.hook: Add a passkey to your Google account
posts.body: The highest-value 60 seconds you'll spend on your accounts today: add a passkey to your Google account. 🔐 A passkey swaps your password for your phone's Face ID or fingerprint — nothing to type, nothing to phish, nothing to leak in the next data breach. Even if someone has your password, they can't get in without your device. Head to your Google account, then Security, then Passkeys. It's the closest thing to un-hackable you'll get for free.
posts.problem: Passwords remain the weakest point in most people's accounts — they get reused, phished, and leaked in breaches, and even a strong one is only as safe as the last site that got hacked.
posts.breakthrough: Adding a passkey to your Google account swaps your password for your phone's Face ID or fingerprint at login — nothing to type, nothing to phish, and nothing useful for an attacker to steal even if a data breach exposes it.
posts.catch: A passkey is tied to your device, so losing your phone without a backup method set up can complicate recovery — it's worth confirming you have a second passkey or recovery option before you rely on it fully.
posts.forYou: This is a genuine 60-second fix: go to your Google Account, then Security, then Passkeys, and turn it on — it's one of the few security upgrades that costs you nothing and meaningfully closes a real attack vector.

posts.hook: Foldables: genuinely useful, or an expensive flex?
posts.body: Samsung's Galaxy Unpacked lands July 22 with the tagline 'a new shape unfolds' — almost certainly the Z Fold 8. 📱 Before the hype cycle spins up, the real question is whether foldables are genuinely better for how you actually use a phone, or a beautiful flex most people justify after the fact.
